focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 11

Flies rose first. They lifted off the windfalls in a slow black veil when Della stepped into the ditch grass beside the road, and the orchard smell came after them: split fruit, old spray lifted fresh by rain, yellow-jacket sugar, and the wet iron smell that had started riding the valley since the dam crews turned the river inside out. Beyond the sagging fence the rows lay half-hidden under sycamore shade, but rot had no need of a clear view. It got to a place faster than grief ever did, and by then it had already slicked her shoes with mashed apples and clay.

She had left the Bledsoe house with a flour sack, a short pry bar, and no explanation worth hearing aloud. June had watched from the pump with her braid half-finished, one ribbon caught between her teeth while she squinted into the morning sun. Ruthie, taking shirts off the line before the heat fixed them stiff with soap, had looked once at Della's hands and once at the sack and said, "If you're set on going there, take the bee net." Then she had passed over an old felt hat with muslin stitched around the brim and gone back to the wash basket. Once Ruthie got tired of pleading, she supplied the useful thing and let a person feel the shame of needing it.

Now Della stood at the gate longer than the lock deserved. Vernon had put a new chain through it, bright enough to catch what little sun made it under the sycamores, and nailed a fresh board on the post in his church hand: WOLFE TRACT. NO TRESPASSING. He had let the side fence go down to fox runs and blackberry cane, but he had kept the front neat where the road might see it. He did with land what he did with most things: kept the front tidy for witnesses and left the rot to work out back.

She could have come with Lucille and a county order, but pa...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...watched you go in."

"Let 'em watch," Della said, though the words came tired.

"That ain't what I said." Ruthie fixed the sheet to the line with two hard bites of the clothespins. "When he hears, he'll come home hot and stupid. What does next week need besides your temper?"

"Silas if he can travel. My copies. Anything left that ties the grave to itself and not to Rafe's want."

June had gone still. That meant she was already choosing. "Then I'll go get Mr. Wynn before Daddy gets back."

Ruthie turned on her at once. "Shoes first. Not those porch slippers. Take the lower path so you don't spend half the day explaining yourself to neighbors."

June wiped her wet hands down her skirt. "I'm not explaining a thing."

Della stepped away from the car. The ground gave a small, mean throb up through her knees. Lucille was still at the wheel, looking straight ahead with the decency of a woman pretending not to witness another family's crack widen. Della shut the door and Lucille drove off in a cough of dust.

Ruthie reached for the oilcloth bundle before Della quite knew she meant to. "Come inside. We aren't leaving those in your apron for him to put his hands on."

It was the first time either of them had said him and meant Rafe plain as a storm front. Della let Ruthie take the papers. The wet smell of laundry, lye, and road dust followed them to the porch while June ran for her shoes and then down the slope toward Silas Wynn's place, long legs eating the yard. By the time Della crossed the threshold, Ruthie had already swept the sugar bowl, the mending basket, and yesterday's almanac off the table to make room for the county copies. The house no longer looked like a house waiting supper out. It looked like a room about to defend itself before the valley got hold of the story.
